common_name: Rose-Painted Calathea (Medallion Plant, Dottie Calathea, Rose-Painted Prayer Plant)

planting.note: Grow in bright indirect light — no direct sun, which bleaches and scorches the patterned leaves. Use well-draining peat-based or peat-free porous mix; keep consistently moist but never waterlogged. Calatheas are sensitive to fluoride in tap water — use rainwater, distilled water, or water that has stood overnight. Maintain high humidity (60%+ preferred). Propagate by dividing rhizomes in spring. The cultivar 'Medallion' (dark green with pink midrib and silver-green margins) and 'Dottie' (nearly black leaves with pink margins) are widely available. All parts non-toxic.

directions: Grow in bright indirect light to dim interior light — Rose-Painted Calathea is one of the most shade-tolerant of the ornamental calatheas and tolerates low-light conditions that would defeat most other foliage plants. Calathea roseopicta is a rhizomatous, clump-forming tropical perennial native to northwestern Brazil and adjacent Colombia in the family Marantaceae, one of the most widely grown and ornamentally important of the approximately 200 Calathea species for indoor and tropical shade garden use. The leaves are large (6–12 inches long), broadly oval, carried upright on graceful petioles, and decorated with some of the most elaborate natural patterns of any houseplant: the upper surface is dark green with a feathered lighter green center stripe, bordered by a band of bright rosy pink or silvery white, then a dark green margin — a concentric color pattern that earned the plant the name 'Rose-Painted Calathea.' The undersides are uniformly deep purple-maroon. Like other Marantaceae members, C. roseopicta exhibits nyctinasty — the leaves fold upward at night to reveal the purple undersides, then open again at daybreak, driven by a circadian rhythm. Multiple named cultivars with varying color expressions are widely available: 'Medallion' (classic pink-banded), 'Dottie' (near-black leaves with bright pink margins), 'Rosey' (silvery-green with pink), and 'Medallion Fusion.' Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Calathea species for indoor and tropical garden use
Royal Horticultural Society — Calathea roseopicta cultivation
Missouri Botanical Garden — Calathea roseopicta species profile
Marantaceae Society — Calathea roseopicta cultivar notes

common_name: Black Velvet Alocasia (Little Queen Alocasia, Black Velvet Elephant Ear)

planting.note: Grow in bright indirect to low-light conditions in well-drained, slightly gritty, aerated potting mix; Alocasia reginula needs better drainage than many aroids — root rot in soggy media is the most common cause of decline. Allow the top inch of soil to dry between waterings. This jewel alocasia stays small (12–18 inches) and is suited to smaller containers. Maintain warm temperatures (65–85°F) and moderate humidity. Propagate by separating offsets (corms) from the parent rhizome. All parts toxic if ingested — calcium oxalate crystals.

directions: Grow in bright indirect light in well-drained, aerated soil — Black Velvet Alocasia is a compact 'jewel' alocasia that performs best in the bright, diffuse light of a shaded indoor or sheltered outdoor position and will not tolerate direct sun, which scorches the velvety leaf surface. Alocasia reginula is a small, rhizomatous aroid native to Borneo (Sabah, Malaysia) in the Araceae family, one of the most coveted of the so-called 'jewel alocasias' — a group of compact, ornamental Alocasia species prized for extraordinary foliar texture and color rather than dramatic size. The leaves are the defining feature: heart-shaped to sagittate, thick, and covered in a dense, velvety, blue-black to dark charcoal-green surface with a distinctive texture resembling crushed velvet — the velvet quality results from tightly packed, densely hairy papillae covering the leaf epidermis. The veins are silver-white to pale silver, creating a bold, contrasting pattern of silver lines on the near-black velvet surface — a combination of texture and color contrast unlike almost any other aroid. The undersides are a deep maroon-purple. The plant remains compact (under 18 inches), making it ideal for terrariums, small containers, and sheltered shaded spots in zone 11-12 tropical gardens. All parts toxic.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Alocasia reginula species profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Jewel Alocasias for tropical collections
International Aroid Society — Alocasia reginula cultivation notes
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Alocasia (toxic to pets)

common_name: Mirror Plant (Copper Alocasia, Red Secret Alocasia, Giant Caladium)

planting.note: Grow in bright indirect light in well-drained, chunky, aerated aroid mix — Alocasia cuprea needs excellent drainage and is prone to root rot in dense, moisture-retaining media. Allow the top 1–2 inches to dry before re-watering. Maintain warm temperatures (65–85°F) and moderate humidity (50%+). The metallic sheen of the leaves is a structural color effect and intensifies in bright indirect light. The common name 'Red Secret' refers to the deep red-purple undersides. Propagate by separating corms. All parts toxic.

directions: Grow in bright indirect light in well-draining, aerated potting mix — Copper Alocasia produces its most intense metallic leaf sheen in bright indirect light and becomes dull in dim conditions while being scorched by any direct sun. Alocasia cuprea is a compact, rhizomatous aroid native to Borneo (Sarawak, Malaysia) in the Araceae family, one of the most visually striking of the jewel alocasias and widely coveted among tropical plant collectors for its extraordinary, metallic, iridescent leaf surface. The leaves are broadly oval to cordate, thick and leathery, and covered with a surface that appears to be hammered copper or burnished metal — an intense, reflective, coppery-bronze to deep bronze-green sheen that seems almost industrial in its metallicity, unlike the texture of any other commonly grown aroid. This metallic appearance is a structural color effect produced by specialized leaf cell architecture that acts as a diffraction grating for light. The deeply sunken primary and secondary veins create a strong, dark grid pattern against the bright metallic surface. The undersides are deep purple-red to maroon (the source of the popular cultivar name 'Red Secret'). An established, well-grown Alocasia cuprea with several large metallic leaves simultaneously is one of the most visually arresting of all tropical foliage plants. All parts contain calcium oxalate crystals and are toxic.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Alocasia cuprea species profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Jewel Alocasias for tropical indoor collections
International Aroid Society — Alocasia cuprea cultivation guide
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Alocasia (toxic to pets)

common_name: Dragon Scale Alocasia (Silver Dragon Alocasia, Baginda Alocasia)

planting.note: Grow in bright indirect light in very well-drained, chunky, perlite-enriched aroid mix; Alocasia baginda is particularly susceptible to root rot — never allow the pot to sit in standing water and ensure the potting medium drains freely. Allow top 1–2 inches to dry before watering. Two cultivars are commonly available: 'Dragon Scale' (dark green with silver-gray scale pattern) and 'Silver Dragon' (pale silver-gray leaves with green venation). Maintain warm temperatures above 60°F. High humidity (60%+) prevents crispy leaf margins. Propagate from corm offsets. All parts toxic.

directions: Grow in bright indirect light — Dragon Scale Alocasia needs good light to develop and maintain its distinctive scale patterning but cannot tolerate direct sun, which causes leaf bleaching and scorching on the sensitive leaf surface. Alocasia baginda is a compact, rhizomatous aroid native to Borneo (Sabah, Malaysia) in the Araceae family, one of the most extraordinary of the jewel alocasias for its unique leaf surface texture — a surface so precisely patterned that it bears a startling resemblance to the overlapping scale armor of a mythological dragon. The leaves are thick, leathery, broadly ovate with a slightly cordate base, and covered with a distinctive bumpy to quilted surface texture: each cell group between the veins forms a slightly raised, convex, scale-shaped unit that overlaps adjacent units in a regular, tile-like pattern — the overall impression is unmistakably of reptilian scales. In the 'Dragon Scale' cultivar, the background color is dark gray-green with silvery-gray scale highlights; in the 'Silver Dragon' form, the entire leaf appears pale silver-gray with green veins — a ghostly, luminous coloration unlike any other plant. The undersides are deep burgundy-purple. Two distinct cultivars circulate in the trade, both of extraordinary ornamental value in terrarium, collector shelf, and indoor tropical garden settings. All parts toxic.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Alocasia baginda species profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Jewel Alocasias for tropical collections
International Aroid Society — Alocasia baginda 'Dragon Scale' and 'Silver Dragon' notes
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Alocasia (toxic to pets)

common_name: Lance-Leaf Begonia (Amphioxus Begonia, Spotted Sarawak Begonia)

planting.note: Grow in bright indirect to medium indirect light in well-drained, humus-rich, porous potting mix with excellent drainage; Begonia amphioxus is a trailing to semi-erect cane begonia best displayed in a hanging basket or allowed to cascade over a pot edge. Maintain consistent moisture but allow the surface to dry slightly between waterings. This species appreciates moderate humidity (50%+) and good air circulation. Propagate from stem cuttings. A terrarium plant in temperate climates; zone 11-12 outdoor shade garden in tropical conditions. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in bright indirect light in moist, well-drained, humus-rich potting mix — Lance-Leaf Begonia performs best in the bright, diffuse light of a shaded indoor position or sheltered tropical shade garden and produces its most vivid leaf spotting in good indirect light. Begonia amphioxus is a small, trailing to semi-erect begonia native to Sarawak, Malaysia (Borneo) in the Begoniaceae family, one of the most visually distinctive of the hundreds of wild Begonia species collected by tropical plant enthusiasts for its remarkable combination of leaf shape and surface pattern. The leaves are the defining feature: narrow, lance-shaped to narrowly ovate (2–4 inches long but only 0.5–1 inch wide — extremely elongated relative to the width), with a strongly tapering, spear-like tip that gives the species its name 'amphioxus' (Greek for 'pointed at both ends'). On this narrow, bright green surface, scattered crimson-red to scarlet spots and blotches of varying sizes are distributed irregularly — the effect is of a thin bright-green ribbon patterned with vivid red paint droplets. The undersides are deep red. The plant also produces small pink flowers in clusters from the leaf axils throughout the growing season. A collector's begonia valued for its completely distinctive and unusual leaf shape — the extreme narrowness distinguishing it from nearly all other cultivated begonia species. Non-toxic to pets.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Begonia amphioxus species profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Collector Begonias for tropical indoor gardens
American Begonia Society — Begonia amphioxus: wild Borneo species
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Begonia (non-toxic to pets)
